value: Angiotensin-converting enzyme inhibitors (ACEi) and angiotensin II receptor blockers (ARBs) are medications primarily used to treat hypertension and heart failure. They work by relaxing blood vessels and decreasing blood volume, which lowers blood pressure and makes it easier for the heart to pump blood.

Use of these medications is extremely common as they are first-line treatments for prevalent cardiovascular and renal conditions. We would expect to identify a very large number of users in primary care, hospital, and insurance claims data.
60 to 70 (The median age reflects the typical age of onset for the main indications for these drugs, such as hypertension, heart failure, and type 2 diabetes.)
45% to 55% (The conditions treated by these drugs are common in both sexes, resulting in a relatively balanced sex distribution among users.)
80% to 95% (Survival is generally high as these drugs manage chronic conditions, although the underlying cardiovascular diseases mean mortality is higher than in the general population.)

A person prescribed an acei/arb will also be in an antihypertensive cohort (100%), and there is very high overlap with cohorts for hypertension (70-90%), dyslipidemia (40-70%), statin use (40-70%), type 2 diabetes (20-40%), heart failure (20-40%), and chronic kidney disease (20-40%), with moderate overlap for cohorts of acute myocardial infarction (20-40%), ischaemic stroke (20-40%), other antihypertensives like beta_blockers or calcium_channel_blockers (20-50%), and aspirin (30-60%).

For chronic conditions like hypertension or type 2 diabetes, the diagnosis often precedes or is concurrent with acei/arb initiation, whereas for acute events like a myocardial infarction or ischaemic stroke, the acei/arb is typically started days to weeks after the event.

hypertension, dyslipidaemia, type 2 diabetes, chronic kidney disease, ischaemic heart disease, heart failure, atrial fibrillation, obesity, peripheral vascular disease, ischaemic stroke. (these comorbidities represent either the primary indications for prescribing acei/arbs or are part of the same spectrum of cardiovascular and metabolic diseases.)

elevated blood pressure, dyspnoea, peripheral oedema, fatigue, chest pain, palpitations, proteinuria, dizziness, headache, decreased exercise tolerance. (these are signs and symptoms of the underlying conditions for which acei/arbs are prescribed, such as hypertension, heart failure, and chronic kidney disease.)

thiazide diuretics, statins, beta-adrenergic blocking agents, calcium channel blockers, aspirin, metformin, direct oral anticoagulants, loop diuretics, proton pump inhibitors, P2Y12 inhibitors. (these medications are frequently co-prescribed to manage associated cardiovascular conditions and risk factors, reflecting guideline-recommended combination therapy.)

Anti-dyslipidaemic medications are a class of drugs used to treat dyslipidaemia, a condition characterised by abnormal levels of lipids such as cholesterol and triglycerides in the blood. This condition is a major risk factor for the development of atherosclerosis and subsequent cardiovascular disease.
The use of anti-dyslipidaemic medication is extremely common, particularly in adults over 40, and these individuals will be frequently identified in primary care, hospital, and insurance claims data.
55 to 65 (Initiation of therapy often occurs in middle to older age as part of primary or secondary prevention for cardiovascular disease, following risk assessment or a cardiovascular event.)
50% to 60% (Men are more likely to be prescribed these medications, reflecting their earlier onset and higher prevalence of cardiovascular disease compared to pre-menopausal women.)

90% to 98% (Survival is generally high as these medications are preventative, with mortality being driven by the underlying cardiovascular risk profile and comorbidities rather than the treatment itself.)

Overlap will be extremely high with dyslipidemia (95-100%) and statin (80-95%) cohorts. Significant overlap is also expected with hypertension (40-70%), antihypertensive medications (40-70%), type 2 diabetes (20-40%), aspirin (30-60%), acute myocardial infarction (15-30%), and ischaemic stroke (10-25%).

For primary prevention, entry into cohorts such as dyslipidemia or hypertension may precede the start of anti-dyslipidaemic therapy by months or years. For secondary prevention, entry into the anti-dyslipidaemic cohort will typically occur within days or weeks after an acute myocardial infarction or ischaemic stroke.

the most common comorbidities are dyslipidemia, hypertension, type 2 diabetes, obesity, ischaemic stroke, acute myocardial infarction, heart failure, and chronic kidney disease. (These medications are central to managing cardiovascular risk, so they are frequently prescribed to people with established cardiovascular diseases or a cluster of risk factors.)

the most common signs and symptoms are largely absent as dyslipidaemia is asymptomatic, but may include those of resultant conditions like acute myocardial infarction or ischaemic stroke. (Dyslipidaemia is a biochemical finding and typically does not cause symptoms itself, so clinical signs only manifest once atherosclerosis has progressed to cause cardiovascular events.)

the most common medications are statins, antihypertensives, aspirin, angiotensin converting enzyme inhibitors/angiotensin receptor blockers, beta blockers, p2y12 inhibitors, calcium channel blockers, and ezetimibe. (These medications are often co-prescribed to provide comprehensive management of the multiple, co-existing risk factors for cardiovascular disease, such as hypertension and thrombosis.)

Anticoagulants are a class of drugs that work to prevent blood coagulation or prolong the clotting time. They are prescribed for the primary and secondary prevention of thromboembolic events arising from conditions such as atrial fibrillation, venous thromboembolism, and the presence of mechanical heart valves.

The use of anticoagulants is very common, particularly in older adults, and these prescriptions are well-captured in both primary and secondary care electronic health records as well as in administrative claims data.
68 to 78 (Individuals are typically initiated on anticoagulants in later life, as the primary indications such as atrial fibrillation and venous thromboembolism increase in incidence with age.)

45% to 55% (The proportion of males and females is expected to be relatively balanced, though it may be slightly higher in males reflecting the sex distribution of some underlying cardiovascular conditions.)
65% to 80% (Survival following initiation of an anticoagulant is highly dependent on the indication and the burden of comorbidities, which are often significant in this patient population.)

A very high overlap (80-100%) is expected with specific anticoagulant classes like warfarin and doacs; high overlap (40-80%) with indications like atrial_fibrillation and ischaemic_stroke, and comorbidities like hypertension; moderate overlap (20-50%) with other cardiovascular medications like statins, beta_blockers, and antihypertensives; and lower overlap (5-20%) with major procedures like percutaneous_coronary_intervention.

Chronic risk factors like hypertension or type_2_diabetes will typically be recorded years before the first anticoagulant prescription, whereas the primary indication, such as atrial_fibrillation or ischemic_stroke, will be recorded concurrently or within days to weeks before initiation.

atrial fibrillation, hypertension, heart failure, type 2 diabetes, chronic kidney disease, dyslipidaemia, ischaemic stroke, acute myocardial infarction, obesity and valve disorder (These comorbidities are frequently observed as they are often the underlying indications for anticoagulation or are common cardiovascular risk factors within the same patient population.)

dyspnoea, chest pain, palpitations, oedema, haematuria, epistaxis, melaena, focal neurological deficit, fatigue and syncope (These represent the signs and symptoms of the underlying conditions requiring treatment, such as atrial fibrillation or venous thromboembolism, or potential side effects of the medication such as bleeding.)

statins, beta-blockers, angiotensin-converting enzyme inhibitors, angiotensin ii receptor blockers, diuretics, proton pump inhibitors, aspirin, clopidogrel, digoxin and metformin (These medications are commonly co-prescribed to manage related cardiovascular conditions and risk factors, or to mitigate side effects of anticoagulant therapy.)

Antihypertensives are a class of drugs used to treat hypertension (high blood pressure). Hypertension is a chronic medical condition in which the blood pressure in the arteries is persistently elevated, a major risk factor for coronary artery disease, stroke, heart failure, and chronic kidney disease.

The use of antihypertensives is extremely common, reflecting the high prevalence of hypertension in the adult population. Cases will be readily identifiable in primary care, hospital, and insurance claims data.
55 to 65 (Individuals are typically diagnosed with hypertension and initiated on treatment in middle to older age, with the prevalence increasing significantly with age.)
50% to 60% (Hypertension is slightly more common in men than women until later in life, after which the prevalence in women increases.)
90% to 95% (Mortality is primarily driven by associated cardiovascular events rather than the hypertension itself, and survival is high with effective blood pressure control.)

Overlap will be extremely high (approaching 100%) with the hypertension cohort, and high (50-100%) with the specific component classes such as acei_arbs, beta_blockers, calcium_channel_blockers, and thiazide_diuretics. High overlap (30-70%) is expected with cohorts for dyslipidaemia, statins, type 2 diabetes, and obesity, and moderate overlap (10-40%) with cohorts for cardiovascular events like acute_mi, heart_failure, and ischemic_stroke.

The antihypertensive cohort entry will typically occur at the same time as, or within days of, entry into the hypertension cohort. Entry into risk factor cohorts like type 2 diabetes or dyslipidaemia can occur years before or around the same time. Entry into cohorts for cardiovascular events like acute_mi, heart_failure, or ischemic_stroke typically occurs several years after the initiation of antihypertensive treatment, representing disease progression.

hypertension, dyslipidaemia, type 2 diabetes, obesity, chronic kidney disease stage 3, atrial fibrillation, heart failure, ischaemic stroke, acute myocardial infarction, coronary artery disease (these conditions share common risk factors with hypertension, or are direct consequences of long-term elevated blood pressure on target organs like the heart, brain, and kidneys.)

elevated blood pressure reading, headache, dizziness, visual disturbances, chest pain, dyspnoea, peripheral oedema, fatigue, palpitations, haematuria (while often asymptomatic, these signs and symptoms can arise from severely elevated blood pressure or from hypertension-related damage to the cardiovascular, renal, and central nervous systems.)

angiotensin-converting enzyme inhibitors, angiotensin ii receptor blockers, calcium channel blockers, thiazide diuretics, beta-blockers, statins, aspirin, oral hypoglycaemics, anticoagulants, insulin (this list includes the major classes of antihypertensive drugs themselves, as well as medications to manage commonly co-occurring conditions like dyslipidaemia, diabetes, and cardiovascular disease risk.)

Aspirin is a nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drug that works by inhibiting platelet aggregation to prevent the formation of blood clots. It is widely used for the secondary prevention of cardiovascular and cerebrovascular events such as heart attacks and strokes.

Aspirin is an extremely common medication, available both over-the-counter and on prescription, and its use is readily identifiable in most healthcare data sources including primary care, hospital, and insurance claims.
65 to 75 (Individuals are typically middle-aged or older when aspirin is initiated for cardiovascular protection, often following a cardiovascular event or when risk factors accumulate.)
50% to 65% (A slightly higher proportion of males is expected as men tend to develop cardiovascular disease at an earlier age than women.)
70% to 85% (Survival is generally good but is lower than in the age and sex matched general population due to the underlying cardiovascular conditions for which aspirin is prescribed.)

Expect very high overlap (60-95%) with cohorts for acute cardiovascular events (acute_mi, ischemic_stroke), procedures (percutaneous_coronary_intervention), cardiovascular risk factors (hypertension, dyslipidemia), and related medications (statin); moderate overlap (20-50%) is expected with conditions like heart_failure and type_2_diabetes, and with other cardiovascular drugs like p2y12_inhibitors and anticoagulants.

Entry into the aspirin cohort is often simultaneous with or days after entry into cohorts for acute events like acute_mi or procedures like percutaneous_coronary_intervention; for chronic conditions like hypertension or type_2_diabetes, the diagnosis is likely to precede aspirin initiation by months or years.

hypertension, dyslipidemia, type 2 diabetes, ischaemic stroke, heart failure, atrial fibrillation, obesity, chronic kidney disease, cartoid artery disease, and acute myocardial infarction (These conditions are common as they are established risk factors for, or consequences of, the atherosclerotic cardiovascular diseases that aspirin is used to prevent.)

angina pectoris, dyspnoea, palpitations, peripheral oedema, intermittent claudication, syncope, fatigue, hemiparesis, dizziness, and dysarthria (These are common symptoms related to the underlying cardiovascular, cerebrovascular, and peripheral artery diseases for which aspirin is indicated.)

statin, acei_arbs, beta_blockers, calcium_channel_blockers, thiazide_diuretics, p2y12_inhibitors, doacs, warfarin, ezetimibe, and pcsk9_inhibitors (These drugs are frequently used alongside aspirin as part of comprehensive guideline-directed management of cardiovascular disease and its risk factors.)

Beta-blockers are a class of medication primarily used to manage cardiovascular conditions such as hypertension, angina, heart failure, and atrial fibrillation. They function by blocking beta-adrenergic receptors, which reduces the effects of adrenaline, leading to a decreased heart rate, lower blood pressure, and reduced cardiac workload.

Beta-blockers are very commonly prescribed drugs, and cohorts of users are readily identifiable in real-world data sources that contain prescription information, such as primary care records and administrative claims data.
55 to 70 (Beta-blockers are typically initiated in middle-aged to older adults for conditions like hypertension, angina, or heart failure, which become more prevalent with increasing age.)
45% to 60% (The use of beta-blockers is common in both men and women, with a slight male predominance often observed due to the higher prevalence of cardiovascular disease in men.)

75% to 90% (Survival for individuals prescribed beta-blockers is largely determined by the severity of the underlying cardiovascular indication, ranging from a good prognosis for simple hypertension to a poorer prognosis for advanced heart failure.)

High overlap (40-80%) is expected with cohorts for hypertension, other antihypertensive medications (acei_arbs, calcium_channel_blockers), and dyslipidemia-related cohorts (statin). Moderate to high overlap (20-60%) is expected with heart_failure, atrial_fibrillation, acute_mi, and related medications like aspirin and anticoagulants. Lower but significant overlap (10-40%) is seen with type_2_diabetes, chronic kidney disease, and revascularisation procedures like percutaneous_coronary_intervention.

For acute events like acute_mi or procedures such as percutaneous_coronary_intervention, beta-blocker initiation is typically concurrent or within days, whereas for chronic conditions like hypertension, beta-blockers may be started months or years after the initial diagnosis, often as an addition to existing treatments.

the most common comorbidities are hypertension, heart failure, coronary artery disease, atrial fibrillation, dyslipidaemia, type 2 diabetes, chronic kidney disease, obesity, ischaemic stroke, and peripheral arterial disease. (these comorbidities are commonly observed as they represent either the primary indications for beta-blocker therapy, such as hypertension and heart failure, or are part of the broader metabolic and cardiovascular risk profile of the patient.)

the most common signs and symptoms are bradycardia, fatigue, dizziness, hypotension, dyspnoea, chest pain, palpitations, peripheral oedema, headache, and cold extremities. (these reflect a mixture of the common side effects of the medication itself, such as bradycardia and fatigue, and the symptoms of the underlying cardiovascular diseases being treated, like dyspnoea and chest pain.)

the most common medications are statins, angiotensin-converting enzyme inhibitors, angiotensin receptor blockers, calcium channel blockers, thiazide diuretics, aspirin, p2y12 inhibitors, direct oral anticoagulants, warfarin, and metformin. (these medications are frequently prescribed alongside beta-blockers as part of established guideline-directed combination therapies for managing complex cardiovascular conditions and their associated risk factors.)

Calcium channel blockers are a class of medicines used to lower blood pressure and treat other cardiovascular conditions such as angina pectoris and arrhythmias. They work by relaxing blood vessels and, for some types, reducing the heart's rate and force of contraction.

Calcium channel blockers are very commonly prescribed medications for chronic conditions, so cases are expected to be frequently identified in primary care electronic health records, hospital data, and insurance claims databases.

55 to 65 (Individuals are typically middle-aged or older when first prescribed calcium channel blockers, as the prevalence of indications like hypertension and coronary artery disease increases significantly with age.)
45% to 55% (The underlying conditions treated, such as hypertension, are common in both men and women, resulting in a relatively balanced sex distribution among users.)

85% to 95% (One-year survival is very high, and five-year survival is also high, with mortality primarily determined by the severity of underlying cardiovascular disease and comorbidities rather than the medication itself.)

A very high overlap (50-80%) is expected with hypertension and antihypertensive cohorts, moderate overlap (20-50%) with cohorts for cardiovascular risk factors like type_2_diabetes, dyslipidemia, and statin, and lower overlap (5-20%) with cohorts for acute cardiovascular events like acute_mi and ischemic_stroke.

A diagnosis of hypertension typically precedes or is recorded within days of the first calcium channel blocker prescription, while other medications like statins or acei_arbs may be started concurrently or within the same year, and an acute event like acute_mi would typically precede initiation if the drug is started post-event.

hypertension, type 2 diabetes, dyslipidaemia, obesity, atrial fibrillation, heart failure, ischaemic stroke, acute myocardial infarction, chronic kidney disease, and carotid artery disease are common comorbidities. (These comorbidities are frequently observed as they share common risk factors with, or are complications of, the cardiovascular conditions for which calcium channel blockers are prescribed.)

peripheral oedema, headache, dizziness, flushing, constipation, palpitations, fatigue, nausea, hypotension, and bradycardia are common signs and symptoms. (These are either common side effects resulting from the drug's vasodilatory mechanism of action or symptoms related to the underlying cardiovascular conditions being treated.)

angiotensin-converting enzyme inhibitors or angiotensin receptor blockers, thiazide diuretics, beta blockers, statins, aspirin, P2Y12 receptor inhibitors, direct oral anticoagulants, warfarin, ezetimibe, and other anti-dyslipidaemia agents are common medications. (These medications are often used in combination with calcium channel blockers as part of a multi-faceted approach to manage hypertension, reduce cardiovascular risk, and treat co-existing conditions.)

Dipyridamole is a phosphodiesterase inhibitor with antiplatelet and vasodilator properties. It is primarily used, often in combination with aspirin, for the secondary prevention of ischaemic stroke and transient ischaemic attacks, and also following heart valve replacement surgery to prevent thromboembolism.

The use of dipyridamole has declined in recent years, often being superseded by other antiplatelet agents like clopidogrel, but it can still be identified in both primary and secondary care data, particularly in older patients with a history of cerebrovascular disease.
65 to 75 (Individuals are typically older as the primary indications for use, such as secondary stroke prevention and heart valve replacement, are most common in the elderly population.)
45% to 55% (A near-equal sex distribution is expected, with a slight male predominance reflecting the epidemiology of cardiovascular and cerebrovascular disease.)
50% to 70% (Survival is moderate and largely dictated by the prognosis of the underlying condition, such as a recent ischaemic stroke or significant cardiovascular disease.)

High overlap is expected with cohorts for ischaemic stroke (60-80%), aspirin (70-90%), antihypertensives (e.g. acei_arbs, beta_blockers, calcium_channel_blockers, thiazide_diuretics; 70-90%), statins (60-80%), and hypertension (70-90%); moderate overlap with atrial_fibrillation (20-40%), type_2_diabetes (20-40%), anticoagulants (e.g. warfarin, doacs; 20-40%), and valve_disorder_excl_endocarditis (10-25%); and low overlap with alternative antiplatelets like p2y12_inhibitors (5-15%).

Initiation of dipyridamole typically occurs days or weeks after an acute event like an ischaemic_stroke or a procedure such as valve replacement (in the valve_disorder_excl_endocarditis cohort). It would therefore be preceded, often by many years, by entry into cohorts for chronic risk factors like hypertension, dyslipidaemia, or type_2_diabetes. Entry into cohorts for other secondary prevention medications like statins or aspirin may occur concurrently with dipyridamole initiation.

hypertension, dyslipidaemia, type 2 diabetes, ischaemic stroke, atrial fibrillation, heart failure, coronary artery disease, carotid artery disease, chronic kidney disease, and obesity are the most common comorbidities. (These comorbidities are common as they represent the typical risk factors and established diseases found in a population requiring secondary prevention for atherosclerotic cardiovascular or cerebrovascular events.)

headache, dizziness, nausea, flushing, diarrhoea, vomiting, myalgia, hypotension, angina pectoris, and rash are the most common signs and symptoms. (This profile includes common adverse effects of the drug itself, such as headache and dizziness, as well as symptoms related to underlying cardiovascular disease like angina pectoris.)

aspirin, statins, angiotensin-converting enzyme inhibitors, angiotensin receptor blockers, beta-blockers, calcium channel blockers, thiazide diuretics, warfarin, direct oral anticoagulants, and P2Y12 inhibitors are the most common medications. (These medications are frequently co-prescribed to manage the spectrum of cardiovascular risk, including hypertension, dyslipidaemia, and other indications for anticoagulation or antiplatelet therapy.)

Direct oral anticoagulants are medications used to prevent and treat thromboembolic events. Their use is indicated in conditions that increase the risk of blood clot formation, such as non-valvular atrial fibrillation, deep vein thrombosis, and pulmonary embolism.

DOACs are very commonly prescribed medications that have increasingly replaced older anticoagulants. Cases are readily identifiable in most real-world data sources that contain prescription information, including primary care, secondary care, and insurance claims data.
70 to 80 (Individuals are typically older adults, as the primary indications for DOACs, such as atrial fibrillation, increase in prevalence with age.)
45% to 55% (The proportion of males and females is expected to be relatively balanced, reflecting the similar overall prevalence of indications for anticoagulation between the sexes.)

60% to 75% (One-year survival is expected to be high (0.90-0.95), with five-year survival being substantially lower as mortality is primarily determined by the underlying cardiovascular conditions and comorbidities rather than the medication itself.)

High overlap (70-90%) with atrial_fibrillation. Moderate to high overlap (40-70%) with hypertension, antihypertensive, acei_arbs, beta_blockers, dyslipidemia, and statin. Moderate overlap (20-40%) with heart_failure, type_2_diabetes, and ckd cohorts. Lower overlap (10-30%) with ischemic_stroke, acute_mi, aspirin, and warfarin (indicating switching). As anticoagulants is a parent class, overlap is 100%.

Entry into condition cohorts such as atrial_fibrillation, hypertension, and heart_failure will precede DOAC initiation by months to years. Entry into acute event cohorts like ischemic_stroke will precede DOAC initiation by days to weeks. Initiation of medications like statin or antihypertensive will typically predate the DOAC. Entry into a warfarin cohort will predate entry into the DOAC cohort for patients who switch therapy.

the most common comorbidities are atrial fibrillation, hypertension, heart failure, type 2 diabetes, dyslipidaemia, chronic kidney disease, ischaemic stroke, acute myocardial infarction, obesity, and valve disorders (These conditions represent the primary indications for anticoagulation (e.g., atrial fibrillation, ischaemic stroke) or are common cardiovascular and metabolic risk factors frequently seen in this patient population.)

the most common signs and symptoms are epistaxis, haematuria, haematoma, gingival bleeding, melaena, haematemesis, haemoptysis, rectal bleeding, dyspepsia, and dizziness (These signs and symptoms primarily relate to the medication's mechanism of action, reflecting an increased risk of bleeding, or common gastrointestinal side effects.)

the most common medications are statins, angiotensin-converting enzyme inhibitors or angiotensin receptor blockers, beta-blockers, calcium channel blockers, thiazide diuretics, aspirin, proton pump inhibitors, P2Y12 inhibitors, digoxin, and metformin (These medications are prescribed to manage the common cardiovascular comorbidities such as hypertension, dyslipidaemia, heart failure, and coronary artery disease.)

Ezetimibe is a lipid-lowering medication that inhibits the intestinal absorption of cholesterol. It is used to treat hypercholesterolaemia, often as an adjunct to statin therapy or as monotherapy in patients intolerant to statins.
Ezetimibe is a commonly prescribed medication for dyslipidaemia. Cases are readily identifiable in primary care prescribing databases, pharmacy claims data, and electronic health records.
55 to 70 (Ezetimibe is typically initiated in middle-aged or older adults for the management of hypercholesterolaemia, often after trials of other lipid-lowering therapies.)
50% to 60% (A slight male predominance is expected, reflecting the generally earlier onset and higher prevalence of cardiovascular disease and its risk factors in men.)
85% to 95% (Mortality is primarily determined by the underlying cardiovascular comorbidities and age rather than the medication itself, which is prescribed for long-term risk reduction.)

high overlap (90-100%) is expected with dyslipidaemia and anti_dyslipidaemia cohorts, and very high overlap (50-80%) with statin as it is often a co-prescription; moderate to high overlap (20-70%) is expected with cohorts for atherosclerotic cardiovascular diseases (e.g., acute_mi, ischemic_stroke), their risk factors (e.g., hypertension, type_2_diabetes, obesity), and their treatments (e.g., antihypertensive, aspirin).

Individuals would typically have a diagnosis of dyslipidaemia at the same time as or just before the first ezetimibe prescription; they would often enter a statin cohort years before ezetimibe, and would typically enter cohorts for risk factors like hypertension or type_2_diabetes several years prior, whereas entry into cohorts for acute events like acute_mi or ischemic_stroke would often precede the initiation of ezetimibe by days to weeks as part of secondary prevention.

dyslipidaemia, hypertension, type 2 diabetes, coronary artery disease, obesity, chronic kidney disease, ischaemic stroke, heart failure, atrial fibrillation, peripheral artery disease (these comorbidities represent the common cardiovascular risk factors and established diseases for which intensive lipid-lowering therapy with ezetimibe is indicated.)

angina pectoris, dyspnoea, peripheral oedema, claudication, xanthelasma, xanthoma (these are not symptoms of the medication but rather of the underlying hypercholesterolaemia and associated atherosclerotic cardiovascular diseases being treated.)

statins, aspirin, angiotensin-converting enzyme inhibitors, beta blockers, calcium channel blockers, thiazide diuretics, metformin, angiotensin ii receptor blockers, proton pump inhibitors, clopidogrel (these medications are frequently co-prescribed with ezetimibe to manage the comprehensive cardiovascular risk profile of patients, including hypertension, diabetes, and thrombosis risk.)

Glycoprotein IIb/IIIa inhibitors are a class of potent intravenous antiplatelet agents used to prevent thrombosis during high-risk acute coronary syndromes. They function by blocking the final common pathway for platelet aggregation, inhibiting the binding of fibrinogen to the glycoprotein IIb/IIIa receptor on the surface of platelets.

These drugs are used exclusively in an acute hospital setting, typically a cardiac catheterisation laboratory, during procedures like percutaneous coronary intervention. They would not be seen in primary care data but should be well-captured in hospital electronic health records or procedural data.
60 to 72 (Individuals receiving these agents are typically older adults, as the underlying condition of acute coronary syndrome is most prevalent in this age group.)
60% to 75% (A higher proportion of males is expected as coronary artery disease has a higher incidence and earlier onset in men compared to women.)

75% to 90% (Survival is determined by the severity of the underlying cardiac event and subsequent cardiovascular health, rather than the drug itself, with most mortality due to recurrent ischaemic events or heart failure.)

Expect high overlap (80-100%) with acute_mi and percutaneous_coronary_intervention; high overlap (70-95%) with aspirin, p2y12_inhibitors, statin, and anticoagulants; moderate overlap (40-70%) with hypertension, dyslipidemia, beta_blockers, and acei_arbs; and lower overlap (10-40%) with type_2_diabetes and heart_failure.

Entry into the gp_iib_iiia cohort is simultaneous with acute_mi and percutaneous_coronary_intervention, typically occurring years after a diagnosis of chronic conditions like hypertension or dyslipidemia, and concurrent with the initiation of long-term secondary prevention medications like statins and p2y12_inhibitors.

the most common comorbidities are hypertension, dyslipidaemia, acute myocardial infarction, type 2 diabetes, coronary artery disease, chronic kidney disease, heart failure, and obesity. (These conditions are either the acute indication for the drug's use or are established risk factors for the development of severe coronary artery disease requiring intervention.)

the most common signs and symptoms are angina pectoris, dyspnoea, diaphoresis, nausea, palpitations, syncope, and pain radiating to the arm or jaw. (These are the characteristic clinical manifestations of an acute coronary syndrome, which is the primary reason for administering a glycoprotein IIb/IIIa inhibitor.)

the most common medications are aspirin, p2y12 inhibitors, statins, anticoagulants, beta-blockers, and angiotensin-converting enzyme inhibitors or angiotensin receptor blockers. (These medications form the standard of care for managing acute coronary syndromes and for the long-term secondary prevention of future cardiovascular events.)

P2Y12 inhibitors are a class of antiplatelet agents that block the P2Y12 receptor on platelets, thereby inhibiting platelet activation and aggregation. They are primarily used for the prevention of atherothrombotic events in patients with acute coronary syndrome or those undergoing percutaneous coronary intervention.

Use of P2Y12 inhibitors is common in patients with acute cardiovascular disease, and these prescriptions will be well-captured in both primary and secondary care electronic health records and insurance claims data.
60 to 70 (The median age reflects the typical age of onset for acute coronary syndromes and other cardiovascular events requiring this type of antiplatelet therapy.)
60% to 75% (A higher proportion of males is expected as the primary indications, such as myocardial infarction, have a higher incidence in men.)
70% to 85% (Survival reflects the underlying cardiovascular risk of the population, with mortality being highest in the first year following an acute event.)

A very high overlap (80-95%) is expected with aspirin, acute_mi, and percutaneous_coronary_intervention cohorts; high overlap (60-90%) with statin, anti_dyslipidaemia, hypertension, acei_arbs, and beta_blockers; moderate overlap (10-40%) with ischaemic_stroke, type_2_diabetes, heart_failure, and ckd cohorts; and lower overlap (5-20%) with anticoagulants.

Entry into the p2y12_inhibitors cohort typically occurs at the same time as, or within days after, entry into acute event cohorts like acute_mi, percutaneous_coronary_intervention, or ischemic_stroke. Entry into chronic comorbidity cohorts such as hypertension, dyslipidemia, or type_2_diabetes usually precedes entry into the p2y12_inhibitors cohort by several years.

the most common comorbidities are hypertension, dyslipidaemia, type 2 diabetes, acute myocardial infarction, ischaemic stroke, heart failure, atrial fibrillation, chronic kidney disease, obesity, and carotid artery disease. (These conditions are common cardiovascular risk factors or co-existing diseases in patients requiring secondary prevention for atherothrombotic events.)

the most common signs and symptoms are chest pain, dyspnoea, palpitations, fatigue, dizziness, peripheral oedema, bleeding, bruising, syncope, and haematuria. (These represent symptoms of the underlying cardiovascular disease being treated or are recognised side effects of potent antiplatelet therapy, such as bleeding.)

the most common medications are aspirin, statins, beta-blockers, angiotensin-converting enzyme inhibitors or angiotensin receptor blockers, proton pump inhibitors, calcium channel blockers, thiazide diuretics, nitrates, and anticoagulants. (These medications form the standard of care for secondary prevention of cardiovascular disease and management of related comorbidities.)

PCSK9 inhibitors are injectable monoclonal antibodies that lower low-density lipoprotein (LDL) cholesterol by preventing the degradation of LDL receptors, thereby increasing their availability to clear LDL from the bloodstream. They are primarily used to treat hypercholesterolaemia in patients at very high cardiovascular risk who are unable to reach their LDL cholesterol goals with statins and/or ezetimibe.

PCSK9 inhibitors are used infrequently compared to statins, typically as second or third-line therapy in specialist settings, but cases should be identifiable in both primary and secondary care electronic health records and insurance claims data.
58 to 68 (Individuals are typically middle-aged or older, as they are prescribed for secondary prevention of atherosclerotic cardiovascular disease or for severe primary hypercholesterolaemia.)
55% to 65% (There is often a slight male predominance, reflecting the higher incidence of atherosclerotic cardiovascular disease in men.)
85% to 95% (Survival is generally high as these medications are used for long-term risk reduction in a high-risk but typically stable population.)

Expect almost 100% overlap with dyslipidemia and anti_dyslipidaemia; very high overlap (70-95%) with statin and hypertension; high overlap (40-80%) with aspirin, p2y12_inhibitors, and acute_mi; and moderate overlap (20-50%) with type_2_diabetes, heart_failure, percutaneous_coronary_intervention, ezetimibe, and obesity.

Entry into the pcsk9_inhibitors cohort typically occurs years after entering cohorts for dyslipidemia, hypertension, and statin use, and often weeks or months after an index event like acute_mi or percutaneous_coronary_intervention, reflecting its use as an add-on or later-line therapy.

dyslipidaemia, hypertension, coronary artery disease, type 2 diabetes, previous acute myocardial infarction, chronic kidney disease, ischaemic stroke, peripheral artery disease, obesity, and heart failure (These comorbidities represent the established atherosclerotic cardiovascular diseases and associated risk factors for which intensive lipid-lowering therapy is indicated.)

hypercholesterolaemia, angina pectoris, dyspnoea, peripheral oedema, claudication, focal neurological deficit, transient ischaemic attack, xanthomas, and arcus senilis (These are not direct effects of the medication but rather the clinical manifestations of the underlying dyslipidaemia and established cardiovascular disease being treated.)

statins, ezetimibe, aspirin, P2Y12 inhibitors, angiotensin-converting enzyme inhibitors, angiotensin receptor blockers, beta-blockers, calcium channel blockers, diuretics, and oral anticoagulants (These medications are co-prescribed to manage the spectrum of cardiovascular risk factors and conditions, with statins and ezetimibe being part of the core lipid-lowering strategy.)

Statins are a class of lipid-lowering medications that inhibit the enzyme HMG-CoA reductase, which plays a central role in the production of cholesterol. They are used for the primary and secondary prevention of cardiovascular disease, which is most commonly caused by atherosclerosis.

Statins are among the most commonly prescribed medications worldwide. Cohorts of people taking statins can be readily identified in all major types of real-world healthcare data, including primary care, hospital, and insurance claims data.

55 to 65 (Statin therapy is typically initiated in middle to older age for the prevention of cardiovascular disease, although it may be started earlier in individuals with familial hypercholesterolaemia or other high-risk conditions.)
50% to 60% (A slight male predominance is expected as cardiovascular disease risk is generally higher in men, particularly at younger ages of initiation.)
90% to 95% (Mortality is driven by the underlying cardiovascular risk profile and comorbidities rather than the medication itself, which is prescribed to improve long-term survival.)

Expect very high overlap (50-90%) with dyslipidemia and anti_dyslipidaemia cohorts; high overlap (20-50%) with hypertension, type_2_diabetes, acute_mi, ischemic_stroke, acei_arbs, antihypertensive, and aspirin cohorts; and moderate overlap (5-20%) with heart_failure, atrial_fibrillation, obesity, ckd, and ezetimibe cohorts.

For primary prevention, statin initiation typically follows a diagnosis of dyslipidemia or hypertension by weeks or months. For secondary prevention, statins are usually started within days of an event like an acute_mi or ischemic_stroke, or a procedure such as percutaneous_coronary_intervention.

hypertension, dyslipidaemia, type 2 diabetes mellitus, obesity, acute myocardial infarction, ischaemic stroke, heart failure, atrial fibrillation, chronic kidney disease, and peripheral artery disease (These conditions are either risk factors that prompt the prescription of a statin for cardiovascular disease prevention or are clinical manifestations of the atherosclerotic disease that statins are intended to manage.)

myalgia, headache, nausea, chest pain, dyspnoea, palpitations, peripheral oedema, fatigue, dizziness, and elevated liver enzymes (The signs and symptoms observed are typically related to the underlying cardiovascular conditions for which statins are prescribed or are recognised side effects of the medication.)

aspirin, angiotensin-converting enzyme inhibitors or angiotensin ii receptor blockers, beta-blockers, calcium channel blockers, thiazide diuretics, p2y12 inhibitors, ezetimibe, oral anticoagulants, metformin, and proton pump inhibitors (These medications are frequently co-prescribed with statins to manage the comprehensive cardiovascular risk profile, including hypertension, thrombosis, and type 2 diabetes mellitus.)

Thiazide diuretics are a class of medications primarily used to treat hypertension (high blood pressure) by promoting the excretion of salt and water from the body, thereby reducing blood volume and vascular resistance. They are also used to manage oedema associated with conditions such as heart failure, liver cirrhosis, and kidney disease.

Thiazide diuretics are one of the most commonly prescribed classes of antihypertensive medications worldwide. Cases can be readily identified in all major types of real-world data, including primary care, hospital records, and insurance claims data.
60 to 75 (Individuals are typically middle-aged or older at the time of their first prescription, reflecting the age-related increase in the prevalence of hypertension and heart failure.)

45% to 55% (The proportion of males is expected to be roughly equal to females, as the primary indication, hypertension, has a similar prevalence between sexes in the age groups typically receiving treatment.)

80% to 95% (Survival is generally high as the medication is often used to manage chronic, stable conditions like hypertension, although this is moderated by its use in patients with heart failure who have a poorer prognosis.)

A very high overlap (approaching 100%) is expected with antihypertensive and hypertension cohorts. High overlap (30-70%) is expected with other first-line antihypertensive medication cohorts (acei_arbs, calcium_channel_blockers), cohorts for cardiovascular risk factors (dyslipidemia, type_2_diabetes, obesity) and their treatments (statin, anti_dyslipidaemia). Moderate overlap (10-40%) is expected with cohorts for established cardiovascular diseases (heart_failure, atrial_fibrillation, acute_mi, ischemic_stroke) and chronic kidney disease stages.

Individuals will typically be diagnosed with hypertension at the same time as or shortly before entering the thiazide diuretics cohort. Entry into the thiazide diuretics cohort often occurs years before entry into cohorts for cardiovascular events like acute_mi or ischemic_stroke. For those on multiple antihypertensives, entry into the thiazide diuretics cohort may occur at the same time as, or before/after, entry into cohorts like acei_arbs or calcium_channel_blockers depending on the treatment strategy.

hypertension, dyslipidaemia, type 2 diabetes mellitus, heart failure, chronic kidney disease, obesity, atrial fibrillation, ischaemic stroke, acute myocardial infarction, valve disorder (These conditions are expected as they are either the primary indications for treatment, such as hypertension and heart failure, or are commonly co-occurring cardiovascular risk factors and diseases.)

hypokalaemia, hyponatraemia, hyperuricaemia, hyperglycaemia, dizziness, orthostatic hypotension, dehydration, muscle cramps, fatigue, increased urination (These are typically not related to the underlying condition, which is often asymptomatic, but are well-known metabolic and haemodynamic side effects of the medication itself.)

angiotensin-converting enzyme inhibitors, angiotensin ii receptor blockers, beta blockers, calcium channel blockers, statins, aspirin, metformin, non-steroidal anti-inflammatory drugs, loop diuretics, potassium supplements (These medications are frequently co-prescribed to manage the primary indication, such as hypertension, or to treat common comorbidities like type 2 diabetes and dyslipidaemia.)

Thrombin inhibitors are a class of anticoagulant medications that act by directly or indirectly inhibiting the enzyme thrombin, a key component of the coagulation cascade. They are used for the prevention and treatment of thromboembolic disorders, such as deep vein thrombosis, pulmonary embolism, and stroke prevention in atrial fibrillation.

Thrombin inhibitors are commonly prescribed medications, particularly the direct oral anticoagulants (DOACs) and heparins. They are readily identifiable in prescribing data from both primary and secondary care settings.
65 to 75 (The use of thrombin inhibitors is most common in older adults, reflecting the age-related increase in the incidence of conditions like atrial fibrillation and venous thromboembolism.)
50% to 60% (The underlying indications for these medications, such as atrial fibrillation, are slightly more common in males.)
50% to 75% (Mortality is determined by the underlying cardiovascular and other comorbidities for which the thrombin inhibitor is prescribed, rather than the medication itself.)

Expect very high overlap with the 'anticoagulants' cohort (approaching 100%) and high overlap with 'doacs' (50-80%), as these are sub- or super-classes. High overlap is expected with cohorts for which thrombin inhibitors are indicated, such as 'atrial_fibrillation' (60-80%) and 'ischemic_stroke' (30-50%). Substantial overlap will also exist with common comorbidity cohorts like 'hypertension' (70-90%), 'dyslipidemia' (50-70%), and 'heart_failure' (30-50%), and associated medication cohorts like 'statin' (50-70%) and 'beta_blockers' (50-70%).

Patients will typically be diagnosed with chronic conditions like 'hypertension' or 'type_2_diabetes' many years before entering the 'thrombin_inhibitors' cohort. Entry into the 'thrombin_inhibitors' cohort often occurs at the same time as, or within days to weeks after, an incident diagnosis of 'atrial_fibrillation', 'acute_mi', or 'ischemic_stroke'. Entry into other medication cohorts like 'statin' or 'acei_arbs' often precedes the initiation of a thrombin inhibitor.

atrial fibrillation, hypertension, heart failure, type 2 diabetes, dyslipidaemia, ischaemic stroke, coronary artery disease, chronic kidney disease, obesity, valve disorder (these comorbidities are either indications for anticoagulation or represent a shared cardiovascular risk profile common in patients requiring these medications.)

bleeding, bruising, haematoma, epistaxis, haematuria, gastrointestinal haemorrhage, melaena, haemoptysis, gingival bleeding, intracranial haemorrhage (these signs and symptoms are typically adverse effects related to the medication's anticoagulant properties, reflecting an increased risk of bleeding.)

statin, beta blockers, angiotensin-converting enzyme inhibitors, angiotensin receptor blockers, calcium channel blockers, thiazide diuretics, aspirin, P2Y12 inhibitors, warfarin, direct oral anticoagulants (these medications are commonly co-prescribed to manage the underlying cardiovascular diseases and risk factors for which thrombin inhibitors are indicated.)

Thrombolytics are a class of drugs used to dissolve blood clots (thrombi) that have formed in blood vessels. They are administered in acute, emergency situations to treat conditions such as ischaemic stroke, myocardial infarction, and massive pulmonary embolism by restoring blood flow.

The use of thrombolytics is relatively infrequent and is confined to emergency hospital settings for specific acute thrombotic events. Therefore, cases will be found in hospital-based data but will be rare in primary care or general population datasets.
65 to 75 (Individuals receiving thrombolytics are typically older adults, as the underlying conditions like stroke and heart attack increase in incidence with age.)
55% to 65% (A slight male predominance is expected as cardiovascular diseases, the primary reason for thrombolytic use, are more common in men.)
40% to 60% (Survival is highly dependent on the underlying acute condition being treated and the success of the intervention, with significant mortality in both the short and long term.)

A very high overlap (70-100%) is expected with acute_mi and ischemic_stroke, as these are primary indications, and with related procedures like percutaneous_coronary_intervention and endovascular_thrombectomy. High overlap (40-80%) is expected with cohorts for common comorbidities (hypertension, dyslipidemia, type_2_diabetes, atrial_fibrillation) and secondary prevention medications (aspirin, p2y12_inhibitors, statin, acei_arbs). Moderate overlap (10-40%) is likely with heart_failure and chronic kidney disease stages.

Entry into the thrombolytics cohort typically occurs at the same time (within hours) as entry into acute_mi, ischemic_stroke, percutaneous_coronary_intervention or endovascular_thrombectomy cohorts. Entry into cohorts for chronic comorbidities like hypertension or type_2_diabetes will predate entry into the thrombolytics cohort by several years, whereas entry into secondary prevention medication cohorts like statin or p2y12_inhibitors will usually occur at the same time or within days after thrombolytic administration.

hypertension, dyslipidaemia, type 2 diabetes mellitus, atrial fibrillation, ischaemic heart disease, peripheral arterial disease, obesity, chronic kidney disease, heart failure, and previous stroke or transient ischaemic attack are frequently seen. (These conditions are all major risk factors for the development of atherothrombotic diseases such as myocardial infarction and ischaemic stroke, which are the primary indications for thrombolytic therapy.)

chest pain, dyspnoea, hemiparesis, dysphasia, altered level of consciousness, syncope, diaphoresis, tachycardia, hypotension, and visual field defects are frequently seen. (These are the classic signs and symptoms of the acute medical emergencies for which thrombolytics are administered, such as myocardial infarction, ischaemic stroke, and pulmonary embolism.)

aspirin, clopidogrel, statins, beta-blockers, angiotensin-converting enzyme inhibitors, heparin, angiotensin ii receptor blockers, warfarin, direct oral anticoagulants, and calcium channel blockers are frequently seen. (These medications are commonly used to manage underlying cardiovascular risk factors before the acute event and for secondary prevention afterwards.)

Warfarin is a vitamin K antagonist which acts as an anticoagulant, preventing the formation of blood clots. It is prescribed for the primary and secondary prevention of thromboembolic events, particularly in individuals with atrial fibrillation, venous thromboembolism, or prosthetic heart valves.

Warfarin is a commonly prescribed medication, though its use has declined with the introduction of direct oral anticoagulants (doacs). Cases are readily identifiable in both primary and secondary care electronic health records and in administrative claims data.
72 to 82 (The indications for warfarin, such as atrial fibrillation and venous thromboembolism, are much more common in older adults, hence the typically elderly age at treatment initiation.)
50% to 60% (The primary indications for warfarin use, such as atrial fibrillation, have a slight male predominance, leading to a small majority of males in typical cohorts.)
55% to 75% (Mortality is not driven by the drug but by the underlying cardiovascular conditions and advanced age of the patient population.)

A warfarin cohort will have extremely high overlap with people with atrial_fibrillation (50-80%), hypertension (60-80%), and dyslipidemia (40-60%), and consequently with users of antihypertensive (60-80%) and anti_dyslipidaemia medications (40-60%); overlap with doacs should be very low (0-5%) as they are therapeutic alternatives, and moderate with aspirin (10-30%) as co-prescription occurs in specific clinical scenarios.

Diagnoses for chronic conditions like hypertension, dyslipidemia, or type_2_diabetes will typically predate warfarin initiation by many years; an incident diagnosis of atrial_fibrillation or ischemic_stroke will typically occur in the days or weeks immediately preceding the first warfarin prescription.

atrial fibrillation, hypertension, ischaemic heart disease, type 2 diabetes mellitus, heart failure, chronic kidney disease, venous thromboembolism, ischaemic stroke, dyslipidaemia, and peripheral vascular disease are all commonly seen (These conditions are either the primary indication for anticoagulation or represent the common cardiovascular risk profile of the individuals requiring this treatment.)

epistaxis, haematuria, haematoma, melaena, rectal haemorrhage, haemoptysis, gingival bleeding, haematemesis, subconjunctival haemorrhage, and ecchymosis are all commonly seen (These signs and symptoms are manifestations of bleeding, which is the principal adverse effect associated with anticoagulant therapy like warfarin.)

ramipril, atorvastatin, bisoprolol, furosemide, aspirin, lansoprazole, amlodipine, simvastatin, digoxin, and bendroflumethiazide are all commonly seen (These medications are typically used to manage the common underlying cardiovascular comorbidities in this patient group, such as hypertension, heart failure, and dyslipidaemia.)

Acute myocardial infarction is a medical emergency that occurs when blood flow to a part of the heart is blocked for a long enough period that part of the heart muscle is damaged or dies. The underlying aetiology is most commonly the rupture of an atherosclerotic plaque in a coronary artery, leading to thrombus formation and vessel occlusion.

Acute myocardial infarction is a common cause of hospital admission and is therefore very frequently seen in hospital-based data sources like electronic health records and insurance claims. It is less likely to be recorded as an incident diagnosis in primary care data.
60 to 70 (The incidence of acute myocardial infarction increases significantly with age, with most first events occurring in individuals over the age of 60.)
60% to 70% (Men have a higher incidence of acute myocardial infarction compared to pre-menopausal women, with this sex difference narrowing in older age groups.)

70% to 85% (Although short-term survival has improved dramatically, mortality in the first year after an acute myocardial infarction is significant, and survival rates continue to decline over the following five years.)

Very high overlap (>80%) is expected with secondary prevention medications like aspirin, p2y12 inhibitors, and statins; high overlap (50-80%) with percutaneous coronary intervention and common risk factors like hypertension and dyslipidaemia; and moderate overlap (20-40%) with type 2 diabetes and heart failure.

Comorbid risk factors like hypertension and type 2 diabetes will typically be recorded years before the acute myocardial infarction, while procedures like percutaneous coronary intervention and medications like thrombolytics will be recorded at the same time, and secondary prevention medications are typically started within days of the event.

the most common comorbidities are hypertension, dyslipidaemia, type 2 diabetes, chronic kidney disease, heart failure, atrial fibrillation, obesity, and ischaemic stroke. (These comorbidities are established risk factors for the development and progression of atherosclerosis, which is the underlying pathological process of most acute myocardial infarctions.)

the most common signs and symptoms are chest pain, dyspnoea, diaphoresis, nausea, vomiting, syncope, palpitations, left arm pain, jaw pain, and epigastric discomfort. (These signs and symptoms are a direct result of myocardial ischaemia and the body's autonomic nervous system response to acute cardiac injury and pain.)

the most common medications are aspirin, p2y12 inhibitors, statins, beta blockers, ace inhibitors or angiotensin ii receptor blockers, anticoagulants, thrombolytics, and glycoprotein iib/iiia inhibitors. (These medications are used acutely to manage the event by restoring blood flow and are continued long-term for secondary prevention of future cardiovascular events.)

Atrial fibrillation is a cardiac arrhythmia characterised by chaotic and rapid electrical impulses in the atria, leading to an irregular and often fast ventricular response. This results from structural and electrical remodelling of the atria, often secondary to underlying cardiovascular disease.

Atrial fibrillation is the most common sustained cardiac arrhythmia, and its prevalence increases with age. Cases are frequently recorded in both primary and secondary care data, often identified through electrocardiograms or clinical presentations.
70 to 80 (The incidence of atrial fibrillation rises sharply with advancing age, with most new diagnoses occurring in older adults.)
55% to 65% (Men have a higher age-adjusted incidence of atrial fibrillation compared to women, though lifetime risk is similar due to women's longer life expectancy.)

60% to 75% (Mortality is higher in individuals with atrial fibrillation compared to the general population, primarily due to an increased risk of stroke, heart failure, and other cardiovascular events.)

Expect high overlap (30-70%) with hypertension, heart failure, type 2 diabetes, and their respective medication cohorts (antihypertensives, beta blockers, acei/arbs, statins); moderate overlap (10-30%) with anticoagulants, dyslipidaemia, ischaemic stroke, and ckd; and lower overlap (<10%) with acute mi, valve disorders and procedural cohorts.

Pre-existing risk factor cohorts such as hypertension, type 2 diabetes, and dyslipidaemia will typically be recorded years before the atrial fibrillation diagnosis. Management cohorts like anticoagulants and beta blockers are usually initiated at the time of or shortly after the atrial fibrillation diagnosis, while consequence cohorts like ischaemic stroke or heart failure often appear after.

the most common comorbidities are hypertension, heart failure, type 2 diabetes, dyslipidaemia, obesity, chronic kidney disease, ischaemic stroke, acute myocardial infarction, and valve disorders. (These comorbidities are common as they share risk factors with atrial fibrillation or can directly contribute to the atrial remodelling that precipitates the arrhythmia.)

the most common signs and symptoms are palpitations, dyspnoea, fatigue, dizziness, chest pain, and syncope. (These symptoms are a direct consequence of the irregular and often rapid heart rate, which compromises cardiac output and efficiency.)

the most common medications are anticoagulants (including doacs and warfarin), beta blockers, antihypertensives (including ace inhibitors, angiotensin receptor blockers, calcium channel blockers, and thiazide diuretics), and statins. (These medications are prescribed for rate or rhythm control and, crucially, for stroke prevention, as well as to manage underlying cardiovascular risk factors.)

Carotid artery disease is a form of atherosclerosis characterised by the build-up of plaque within the carotid arteries, the primary vessels supplying blood to the brain. This stenosis, or narrowing, can reduce cerebral blood flow and act as a source for emboli, leading to ischaemic stroke.

Carotid artery disease is a common condition, particularly in older adults with cardiovascular risk factors, and its prevalence increases with age. Cases are readily identifiable in both primary and secondary care data through diagnostic codes for the disease or its consequences, such as stroke, and records of relevant imaging or surgical procedures.
68 to 75 (The disease is strongly age-dependent, with most individuals being diagnosed after the age of 65 following the slow progression of atherosclerosis over many decades.)
55% to 65% (Men tend to be diagnosed at a younger age than women, leading to a slight male predominance in most study cohorts.)
70% to 85% (Mortality is primarily driven by major cardiovascular events, such as myocardial infarction and stroke, reflecting the systemic nature of atherosclerotic disease.)

Expect very high overlap (70-95%) with cohorts for anti-dyslipidaemia (especially statin), antihypertensives, and aspirin; high overlap (60-90%) with hypertension and dyslipidaemia; and moderate overlap with type 2 diabetes (20-40%), acute myocardial infarction (20-40%), ischaemic stroke (20-40%), percutaneous coronary intervention or coronary artery bypass graft (20-40%), and thromboendarterectomy or stenting of extracranial arteries (10-40%).

Individuals are typically diagnosed with risk factor cohorts such as hypertension, dyslipidaemia, and type 2 diabetes many years prior to the carotid artery disease diagnosis. The carotid diagnosis may be concurrent with an ischaemic stroke or acute myocardial infarction, or it may precede it. Definitive treatments such as thromboendarterectomy or stenting will occur after the diagnosis has been established.

hypertension, dyslipidaemia, ischaemic stroke, type 2 diabetes, acute myocardial infarction, atrial fibrillation, heart failure, obesity, and chronic kidney disease are frequently seen. (These comorbidities are common as they share the same underlying pathophysiological mechanisms and risk factors as carotid artery disease, such as atherosclerosis and vascular inflammation.)

carotid bruit, amaurosis fugax, transient ischaemic attack, dysarthria, unilateral weakness, unilateral sensory loss, aphasia, and facial droop are frequently seen. (These are typically neurological and result from thromboembolic events causing temporary or permanent ischaemia in the brain or retina supplied by the affected artery.)

aspirin, statins, clopidogrel, angiotensin-converting enzyme inhibitors, angiotensin receptor blockers, beta-blockers, and calcium channel blockers are frequently seen. (This medication profile reflects treatments to prevent platelet aggregation, manage dyslipidaemia and hypertension, and reduce overall cardiovascular risk.)

Dyslipidaemia is characterised by abnormal levels of lipids, such as cholesterol and triglycerides, in the blood. It can be primary, caused by genetic mutations, or more commonly secondary, resulting from lifestyle factors like diet and inactivity or other medical conditions such as diabetes.

Dyslipidaemia is a very common condition and a major modifiable risk factor for cardiovascular disease. It is readily identifiable in primary and secondary care data through diagnosis codes, lipid panel laboratory results, and prescriptions for lipid-lowering therapies.
45 to 60 (The prevalence of dyslipidaemia increases with age, with incident cases commonly diagnosed in middle-aged or older adults during routine health screening.)
50% to 60% (Men tend to have a higher prevalence of dyslipidaemia than pre-menopausal women, but the incidence in women increases significantly after the menopause.)

90% to 98% (Survival is generally high as dyslipidaemia is a chronic risk factor rather than an acute fatal condition; however, mortality is increased due to its association with atherosclerotic cardiovascular disease.)

Expect very high overlap (70-100%) with lipid-lowering medication cohorts (anti_dyslipidaemia, statin), high overlap (30-70%) with cohorts for common comorbidities like hypertension, type_2_diabetes, and obesity, and moderate overlap (10-40%) with cohorts for atherosclerotic events (acute_mi, ischemic_stroke) and related procedures.

Dyslipidaemia is a chronic risk factor, so its diagnosis would typically precede entry into cohorts for cardiovascular events (e.g., acute_mi, ischemic_stroke) and revascularisation procedures by several years. For other risk factor cohorts (hypertension, type_2_diabetes, obesity), entry may be concurrent or within a few years of the dyslipidaemia diagnosis. Entry into medication cohorts like statin would typically occur at or soon after the dyslipidaemia diagnosis.

hypertension, type 2 diabetes, obesity, acute myocardial infarction, ischaemic stroke, heart failure, atrial fibrillation, carotid artery disease, chronic kidney disease stage 1 and chronic kidney disease stage 2. (These conditions share common risk factors and pathophysiological pathways, such as metabolic syndrome, insulin resistance, and inflammation, which contribute to the development of atherosclerotic cardiovascular disease.)

xanthomas, xanthelasma, arcus senilis, angina pectoris, claudication, transient ischaemic attack, stroke, myocardial infarction, pancreatitis and shortness of breath. (Dyslipidaemia is frequently asymptomatic and is often identified through screening, with signs and symptoms typically arising from its complications such as atherosclerosis or, in severe cases, from the deposition of lipids in tissues.)

statins, ezetimibe, pcsk9 inhibitors, aspirin, angiotensin converting enzyme inhibitors or angiotensin receptor blockers, beta-adrenergic blocking agents, calcium channel blockers, thiazide diuretics, warfarin and direct oral anticoagulants. (These medications include lipid-lowering therapies to directly manage dyslipidaemia and drugs to manage common co-existing cardiovascular risk factors and diseases like hypertension and thrombosis.)

Endocarditis is an infection of the endocardium, the heart's inner lining, most commonly affecting the heart valves. It is typically caused by bacteria, fungi, or other microorganisms entering the bloodstream and attaching to damaged heart tissue or prosthetic devices.

Endocarditis is an uncommon but life-threatening condition, and because it almost always requires hospitalisation for diagnosis and treatment, cases are readily identifiable in hospital-based data but will be rare in purely primary care data.
55 to 70 (The incidence of endocarditis increases with age, with most cases occurring in individuals over 50 years old, often associated with degenerative valve disease or prosthetic valves.)
60% to 75% (A male predominance is consistently observed, with men being affected approximately twice as often as women.)
50% to 65% (Mortality from endocarditis remains high despite medical and surgical advances, with significant mortality both during the initial hospitalisation and in the years following.)

Significant overlap (20-60%) is expected with cohorts for pre-existing cardiovascular conditions and risk factors like valve disorder, heart failure, hypertension, and chronic kidney disease, and their corresponding medication cohorts; overlap with acute events like ischemic stroke may be lower (5-15%) but clinically important.

Individuals typically enter cohorts for chronic risk factors such as valve disorders or hypertension many years prior to the endocarditis diagnosis, whereas entry into cohorts for acute complications like heart failure or ischemic stroke often occurs concurrently or within weeks of the endocarditis event.

valve disorder, heart failure, atrial fibrillation, hypertension, type 2 diabetes, chronic kidney disease, coronary artery disease, malignancy, rheumatic heart disease, intravenous drug use (These comorbidities are frequently observed as they either represent direct risk factors for developing endocarditis, such as pre-existing valve damage, or reflect a patient population with increased susceptibility to severe infections.)

fever, heart murmur, fatigue, chills, shortness of breath, myalgia, arthralgia, night sweats, weight loss, embolic phenomena (The clinical presentation is highly variable, reflecting a combination of systemic infection, immunological responses, and cardiac or embolic complications.)

vancomycin, gentamicin, ceftriaxone, furosemide, ramipril, bisoprolol, aspirin, warfarin, amoxicillin, rifampicin (Medication history is characterised by prolonged courses of high-dose intravenous antibiotics for treatment, alongside therapies for managing common cardiovascular comorbidities like heart failure and hypertension.)

Heart failure is a clinical syndrome caused by a structural or functional cardiac abnormality, resulting in reduced cardiac output and/or elevated intracardiac pressures at rest or during stress. The most common causes are coronary artery disease, hypertension, and valvular heart disease.

Heart failure is a common condition, particularly among older adults, and its prevalence is increasing globally. As a condition requiring long-term management and frequent hospitalisations, cases are readily identifiable in both primary care and hospital administrative data.
75 to 85 (The incidence and prevalence of heart failure increase steeply with age, with the majority of new diagnoses occurring in individuals over the age of 70.)
50% to 60% (Overall, there is a slight male predominance in heart failure incidence, although women comprise a substantial proportion of prevalent cases due to their longer life expectancy.)
40% to 60% (Despite significant advances in treatment, the prognosis for heart failure remains poor, with mortality rates comparable to some common cancers.)

Expect very high overlap (60-90%) with hypertension and key medication cohorts (acei_arbs, beta_blockers, thiazide_diuretics), and high overlap (30-60%) with atrial_fibrillation, acute_mi, type_2_diabetes, chronic kidney disease, statin, anticoagulant, and aspirin cohorts.

Causal risk factors like hypertension and type_2_diabetes typically predate the heart failure diagnosis by many years, whereas an acute_mi may occur concurrently or precede it. Guideline-directed medications for heart failure, such as acei_arbs and beta_blockers, are typically initiated at or shortly after diagnosis, while medications for comorbidities like statins may have been started earlier.

the most common comorbidities are hypertension, atrial fibrillation, type 2 diabetes, dyslipidaemia, chronic kidney disease, acute myocardial infarction, obesity, and valve disorders. (These comorbidities are frequently observed as they are either direct causes of heart failure, such as hypertension or myocardial infarction, or they share common risk factors and pathophysiological pathways.)

the most common signs and symptoms are dyspnoea, orthopnoea, peripheral oedema, fatigue, paroxysmal nocturnal dyspnoea, elevated jugular venous pressure, and pulmonary crackles. (These clinical features arise from the two primary pathophysiological consequences of heart failure: systemic fluid retention and congestion, and inadequate perfusion of tissues and organs.)

the most common medications are ace inhibitors and angiotensin receptor blockers, beta blockers, thiazide diuretics, statins, anticoagulants, aspirin, and direct oral anticoagulants. (This medication profile reflects guideline-directed medical therapy to improve cardiac function and survival, alongside treatments for highly prevalent comorbidities such as ischaemic heart disease and atrial fibrillation.)

Hypertension, or high blood pressure, is a chronic medical condition in which the force of the blood against the artery walls is consistently too high. The vast majority of cases are 'primary' or 'essential' hypertension, which has no identifiable cause and is thought to be linked to genetics, diet, and lifestyle, while a smaller proportion are 'secondary' to other conditions such as kidney disease or endocrine disorders.

Hypertension is extremely common, affecting over a quarter of adults globally, and its prevalence increases with age. As a frequently managed condition in primary care and a common comorbidity in hospitalised patients, cases are readily identifiable in both primary and secondary care real-world data.
50 to 65 (The incidence of hypertension rises steadily with age, with most new diagnoses occurring in middle-aged and older adults.)
45% to 55% (At younger ages, hypertension is more common in men, but the prevalence in women increases significantly after menopause, leading to a relatively balanced sex distribution overall.)
90% to 95% (Although hypertension is a major risk factor for life-threatening events like heart attack and stroke, it is manageable with treatment, and five-year survival after diagnosis is high.)

A high degree of overlap is expected with medication cohorts such as antihypertensive (70-90%), acei_arbs (40-60%), calcium_channel_blockers (30-50%), thiazide_diuretics (20-40%), statin (50-70%), and anti_dyslipidaemia (50-70%). Significant overlap is also expected with comorbid condition cohorts, including dyslipidemia (50-70%), obesity (40-60%), type_2_diabetes (30-50%), and various stages of ckd (20-40%). Overlap with cohorts for acute events like acute_mi or ischemic_stroke, or related procedures like percutaneous_coronary_intervention, would be lower (10-30%), representing the subset of patients who have experienced these sequelae.

Diagnosis of hypertension typically precedes entry into cohorts for its complications (e.g., acute_mi, heart_failure, ischemic_stroke, ckd stages) by several years. Entry into medication cohorts (e.g., antihypertensive, acei_arbs, statin) usually occurs concurrently with or shortly after the hypertension diagnosis, within days to months. Entry into cohorts for other chronic risk factors like type_2_diabetes, dyslipidemia, and obesity often occurs around the same time, typically within a few years before or after the hypertension diagnosis, as part of a developing metabolic syndrome profile.

dyslipidaemia, obesity, type 2 diabetes mellitus, chronic kidney disease, ischaemic heart disease, atrial fibrillation, heart failure, cerebrovascular disease, peripheral arterial disease, and gout (These conditions often share underlying risk factors with hypertension, such as metabolic syndrome, or develop as a direct consequence of long-term damage caused by elevated blood pressure.)

elevated blood pressure reading, headache, dizziness, blurred vision, shortness of breath, epistaxis, palpitations, chest pain, tinnitus, and fatigue (Hypertension is frequently asymptomatic and detected during routine screening, with symptoms typically only appearing when blood pressure is very high or when complications have developed.)

ramipril, amlodipine, indapamide, losartan, bisoprolol, atorvastatin, simvastatin, aspirin, bendroflumethiazide, and candesartan (These medications are prescribed to lower blood pressure and manage associated cardiovascular risk factors like high cholesterol, thereby reducing the likelihood of future cardiovascular events.)

Ischemic stroke results from an occlusion of a blood vessel supplying the brain, leading to a loss of brain function in the affected area. The occlusion is most commonly caused by a thrombus forming on an atherosclerotic plaque or an embolus originating from the heart or a large artery.

Ischemic stroke is a common and serious medical condition, and is a leading cause of long-term disability; cases are readily identifiable in both primary and secondary care electronic health records, as well as in insurance claims data.
70 to 80 (The incidence of ischemic stroke increases substantially with age, with the majority of events occurring in individuals older than 65 years.)
50% to 55% (Men have a slightly higher age-adjusted incidence of ischemic stroke than women, though women have more strokes overall due to their longer life expectancy.)
35% to 55% (Mortality is highest in the first 30 days and first year post-stroke, and is strongly influenced by the initial severity of the stroke, the patient's age, and their comorbidities.)

a high degree of overlap is expected with cohorts for risk factors like hypertension (60-80%) and their treatments like antihypertensives (60-80%), as well as cohorts for other preventative medications like statins (60-80%) and aspirin (50-70%); moderate overlap is seen with atrial fibrillation (15-30%) and anticoagulants (15-30%), while overlap with acute treatments like thrombolytics (5-15%) and endovascular thrombectomy (5-15%) is lower.

risk factor cohorts such as hypertension and type 2 diabetes are typically entered years before the ischemic stroke cohort, whereas cohorts for acute treatments like endovascular thrombectomy or thrombolytics are entered at the same time; cohorts for secondary prevention medications, such as statins or p2y12 inhibitors, are typically entered within days following the stroke diagnosis.

hypertension, dyslipidemia, atrial fibrillation, type 2 diabetes, obesity, heart failure, carotid artery disease, acute myocardial infarction, chronic kidney disease stage 3, chronic kidney disease stage 4 (these conditions are major vascular risk factors that promote the underlying disease processes of atherosclerosis and thromboembolism that lead to ischemic stroke.)

hemiparesis, hemiplegia, aphasia, dysarthria, unilateral facial weakness, ataxia, homonymous hemianopia, hemispatial neglect, altered consciousness, dysphagia (these signs and symptoms represent focal neurological deficits that correspond to the specific area of the brain that has been damaged by the lack of blood flow.)

statin, aspirin, p2y12 inhibitors, anticoagulants, ace inhibitors or angiotensin ii receptor blockers, calcium channel blockers, beta blockers, thiazide diuretics, thrombolytics, dipyridamole (these medications are central to the acute treatment of stroke and for secondary prevention by controlling risk factors and preventing the formation of new blood clots.)

Type 2 diabetes mellitus is a metabolic disorder characterised by high blood sugar, insulin resistance, and relative insulin deficiency. The condition develops from a combination of genetic and lifestyle factors.
Type 2 diabetes is a very common chronic condition that is frequently managed across primary and secondary care, so cases should be readily identifiable in most types of real-world healthcare data.
45 to 65 (The condition typically develops in adults over the age of 45, but it is increasingly seen in younger adults, adolescents, and children.)
50% to 55% (There is a slight male predominance in the prevalence of type 2 diabetes globally.)
85% to 95% (Mortality is higher than in the general population, primarily due to an increased risk of cardiovascular disease and other long-term complications.)

High overlap (50-80%) is expected with cohorts for hypertension, dyslipidaemia, and obesity, and their corresponding medication cohorts such as antihypertensives, statins, and acei_arbs. Moderate overlap (10-40%) is expected with established cardiovascular disease cohorts like acute_mi, heart_failure, ischemic_stroke, and chronic kidney disease. Lower overlap (<10%) is expected with cohorts for more specific procedures or less common conditions like valve disorders or endocarditis.

Individuals often have pre-existing hypertension, dyslipidaemia, or obesity for years before their type 2 diabetes diagnosis. The diagnosis of type 2 diabetes frequently triggers initiation of medications like statins or acei_arbs. Major cardiovascular events like acute_mi or ischemic_stroke, and the development of significant chronic kidney disease, typically occur 5-15 years after the initial diabetes diagnosis.

hypertension, dyslipidaemia, obesity, chronic kidney disease, heart failure, atrial fibrillation, acute myocardial infarction, ischaemic stroke, and coronary artery disease.
